modes.label: What Americans with a view said

metrics.label: The U.S. should have used military force
The U.S. should have used diplomacy and sanctions
The U.S. should have taken a less involved role in the situation with Iran
The U.S. should have used diplomacy and sanctions + The U.S. should have taken a less involved role in the situation with Iran

military_force.label: Soccer Moms
military_force.subtitle: Female, Suburban, College completed or Graduate degree, White
military_force.rationale: The single most-watched voter group in American politics since the 1990s. Swung toward Democrats in 2018/2020, shifted back in parts of 2024. Their education level, suburban geography, and sensitivity to tone/culture-war issues make them the ultimate bellwether for competitive House and Senate races nationwide.

military_force.label: White Working Class
military_force.subtitle: White, No college
military_force.rationale: The realignment story of the last decade. This group's shift from Obama to Trump remade the electoral map. Any erosion or consolidation in this bloc makes front-page news because it directly determines outcomes in PA, MI, WI, and Sun Belt exurbs.

military_force.label: Barstool Conservatives
military_force.subtitle: Male, Gen X or Millennials, No college or College completed, Republican or Other party
military_force.rationale: Culturally conservative but socially libertarian — anti-woke but pro-choice, pro-legal weed, pro-gambling. Credited with helping propel Trump to victory in 2024 through alternative media (Rogan, Barstool Sports, UFC). Not church-right but vibes-right. Their turnout and enthusiasm is a leading indicator of populist energy, and both parties are now scrambling to understand them.

military_force.label: Disaffected Young Men
military_force.subtitle: Male, Gen Z or Millennials, Other party affiliation
military_force.rationale: The emerging wildcard of American elections. Low-propensity, politically homeless, over-indexed on alternative media. Both parties competing for them. Their turnout alone can shift margins by 2-4 points in swing states.

military_force.label: Black Women of Faith
military_force.subtitle: Female, Black, Protestant, Democrat
military_force.rationale: The most reliable voting bloc in the Democratic coalition. When their turnout dips even slightly, Democrats lose. Media tracks their enthusiasm as a proxy for the health of the entire Democratic operation.

military_force.label: Hispanic Working Class
military_force.subtitle: Male, Hispanic, No college
military_force.rationale: The fastest-moving demographic in American politics. Their rightward shift in 2020 and 2024 caught both parties off guard. This group's trajectory determines whether the Sun Belt stays competitive or locks in for one party.

military_force.label: Evangelical Republicans
military_force.subtitle: Republican, Protestant
military_force.rationale: The floor of the Republican coalition. Their mobilization determines whether GOP candidates hit their targets in virtually every state. Post-Dobbs, media watches whether this group stays energized or fractures over candidate quality.

military_force.label: MAGA Country
military_force.subtitle: Rural, Republican, White, No college
military_force.rationale: The margin machine for Republicans. Their ever-increasing turnout in non-metro areas offsets Democratic gains in suburbs. Media covers them as the cultural heartbeat of the populist right. Any sign of turnout fatigue here is a major story.

military_force.label: TikTok Left
military_force.subtitle: Gen Z, Democrat
military_force.rationale: The energy wing of the Democratic base. Their enthusiasm on climate, student debt, and social issues translates to volunteer hours, small-dollar donations, and turnout in college towns. When disengaged, Democratic margins collapse.

military_force.label: Costco Centrists
military_force.subtitle: Suburban, Other party affiliation
military_force.rationale: The persuadable middle that both parties need. Not ideological, but they vote. Media uses them as the 'normal person' stand-in — decides based on gas prices, grocery costs, and vibes, not policy papers.

military_force.label: Tradwifes
military_force.subtitle: Female, Millennials or Gen Z, Republican, Protestant or Catholic, Suburban or Rural
military_force.rationale: A culturally ascendant archetype driven by social media. Embraces traditional gender roles, homemaking aesthetics, and conservative family values. Gained massive visibility in 2024 as a cultural counterpoint to progressive feminism. Media watches them because the tradwife trend was seen as a leading indicator of Trump's win among white women.

military_force.label: Young Black Men
military_force.subtitle: Male, Black, Gen Z
military_force.rationale: A small but symbolically enormous group. Any movement away from Democrats gets massive media coverage because it threatens a core assumption of the Democratic coalition. Even marginal shifts carry outsize narrative weight.

military_force.label: Country Club Republicans
military_force.subtitle: Republican, Suburban, Rich (125k+), College completed or Graduate degree
military_force.rationale: The Republican voters most likely to defect on candidate quality. Their movement in wealthy suburbs (Bucks County, Maricopa, Gwinnett) decided multiple swing states. If they hold for the GOP, the party is strong.

military_force.label: Latte Liberals
military_force.subtitle: Democrat, Urban, Graduate/professional degree
military_force.rationale: The ideological anchor of the Democratic Party. Highly engaged, high-turnout, and the source of most progressive policy energy. When fired up, Democratic campaigns have money and volunteers.

military_force.label: Hispanic Soccer Moms
military_force.subtitle: Female, Hispanic, Suburban, College completed
military_force.rationale: An emerging swing bloc. More moderate than progressive activists, more engaged than working-class Hispanic men. As Sun Belt suburbs diversify, this group increasingly determines outcomes in AZ, NV, and TX.

military_force.label: Tech Bros
military_force.subtitle: Male, Gen Z or Millennials, Other party affiliation, Above median or Rich income
military_force.rationale: A new force in campaign finance and online discourse. Mobilized around deregulation, crypto, and anti-establishment messaging. Their money and meme energy can shift narratives quickly.

military_force.label: Kennedy Catholics
military_force.subtitle: Catholic, Suburban or Urban
military_force.rationale: The original swing demographic in American politics. White Catholics lean R, Hispanic Catholics are contested. Cross-pressured on abortion, immigration, and social welfare. Distributed across every swing state.

military_force.label: Red State Women
military_force.subtitle: Female, White, No college, Republican, Suburban or Rural
military_force.rationale: Often overlooked in media's focus on college-educated suburban women. They are a massive and growing part of the Republican base. Their concerns — inflation, safety, family affordability — drive GOP messaging. Trump gained among them in 2024.

military_force.label: Boomer Democrats
military_force.subtitle: Boomers+, Democrat, College completed or Graduate degree
military_force.rationale: The reliable turnout machine of the Democratic Party — they actually show up in midterms. Often overlooked for flashier demographics, but their consistency keeps Democrats competitive in off-year elections.

military_force.label: Blue-Collar Men
military_force.subtitle: Male, White, No college, Poor or Below median income, Suburban or Rural
military_force.rationale: The kitchen-table economics voter. Cares about wages, trade, and cost of living more than culture war. Overlaps with but is distinct from Barstool Conservatives — these guys are family-first, not libertine. Their economic anxiety is a recurring media theme.

military_force.label: GenX Forgotten Middle
military_force.subtitle: GenX, Other party affiliation, Suburban
military_force.rationale: Perpetually overlooked in media that ping-pongs between Boomers and Gen Z. But GenX is in peak earning and voting years, and their independent streak makes them genuinely persuadable.

military_force.label: Secular Suburbanites
military_force.subtitle: Suburban, Not religious, College completed or Graduate degree
military_force.rationale: The fastest-growing religious demographic is no religion at all. As 'nones' concentrate in suburbs, they look like a coherent voting bloc — socially liberal, fiscally moderate, allergic to culture-war messaging.

military_force.label: Poor Rural Democrats
military_force.subtitle: Democrat, Rural, Poor (<35k)
military_force.rationale: A shrinking but real constituency — complicates the narrative that rural = Republican. Concentrated in the Deep South Black Belt, Appalachian pockets, and Native communities. Mobilization matters in close Senate races.

military_force.label: Six-Figure Socialists
military_force.subtitle: Democrat, Urban, Rich (125k+), Graduate/professional degree
military_force.rationale: The fundraising engine of the progressive movement. Their policy preferences drive Democratic platform priorities disproportionately. Their disconnect from working-class voters is itself a recurring story.

military_force.label: Black Rural Working Class
military_force.subtitle: Black - rural - no college
military_force.rationale: An overlooked but consequential bloc in the rural South and Black Belt. Their political weight can be felt in tight races across Georgia, North Carolina, Alabama, and Mississippi.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Country Club Republicans
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Republican, Suburban, Rich (125k+), College completed or Graduate degree
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: The Republican voters most likely to defect on candidate quality. Their movement in wealthy suburbs (Bucks County, Maricopa, Gwinnett) decided multiple swing states. If they hold for the GOP, the party is strong.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Latte Liberals
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Democrat, Urban, Graduate/professional degree
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: The ideological anchor of the Democratic Party. Highly engaged, high-turnout, and the source of most progressive policy energy. When fired up, Democratic campaigns have money and volunteers.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Hispanic Soccer Moms
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Female, Hispanic, Suburban, College completed
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: An emerging swing bloc. More moderate than progressive activists, more engaged than working-class Hispanic men. As Sun Belt suburbs diversify, this group increasingly determines outcomes in AZ, NV, and TX.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Tech Bros
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Male, Gen Z or Millennials, Other party affiliation, Above median or Rich income
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: A new force in campaign finance and online discourse. Mobilized around deregulation, crypto, and anti-establishment messaging. Their money and meme energy can shift narratives quickly.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Kennedy Catholics
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Catholic, Suburban or Urban
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: The original swing demographic in American politics. White Catholics lean R, Hispanic Catholics are contested. Cross-pressured on abortion, immigration, and social welfare. Distributed across every swing state.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Red State Women
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Female, White, No college, Republican, Suburban or Rural
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: Often overlooked in media's focus on college-educated suburban women. They are a massive and growing part of the Republican base. Their concerns — inflation, safety, family affordability — drive GOP messaging. Trump gained among them in 2024.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Boomer Democrats
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Boomers+, Democrat, College completed or Graduate degree
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: The reliable turnout machine of the Democratic Party — they actually show up in midterms. Often overlooked for flashier demographics, but their consistency keeps Democrats competitive in off-year elections.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Blue-Collar Men
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Male, White, No college, Poor or Below median income, Suburban or Rural
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: The kitchen-table economics voter. Cares about wages, trade, and cost of living more than culture war. Overlaps with but is distinct from Barstool Conservatives — these guys are family-first, not libertine. Their economic anxiety is a recurring media theme.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: GenX Forgotten Middle
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: GenX, Other party affiliation, Suburban
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: Perpetually overlooked in media that ping-pongs between Boomers and Gen Z. But GenX is in peak earning and voting years, and their independent streak makes them genuinely persuadable.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Secular Suburbanites
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Suburban, Not religious, College completed or Graduate degree
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: The fastest-growing religious demographic is no religion at all. As 'nones' concentrate in suburbs, they look like a coherent voting bloc — socially liberal, fiscally moderate, allergic to culture-war messaging.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Poor Rural Democrats
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Democrat, Rural, Poor (<35k)
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: A shrinking but real constituency — complicates the narrative that rural = Republican. Concentrated in the Deep South Black Belt, Appalachian pockets, and Native communities. Mobilization matters in close Senate races.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Six-Figure Socialists
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Democrat, Urban, Rich (125k+), Graduate/professional degree
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: The fundraising engine of the progressive movement. Their policy preferences drive Democratic platform priorities disproportionately. Their disconnect from working-class voters is itself a recurring story.

diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.label: Black Rural Working Class
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.subtitle: Black - rural - no college
diplomacy_plus_less_involved_norm.rationale: An overlooked but consequential bloc in the rural South and Black Belt. Their political weight can be felt in tight races across Georgia, North Carolina, Alabama, and Mississippi.

unsure.label: Soccer Moms
unsure.subtitle: Female, Suburban, College completed or Graduate degree, White
unsure.rationale: The single most-watched voter group in American politics since the 1990s. Swung toward Democrats in 2018/2020, shifted back in parts of 2024. Their education level, suburban geography, and sensitivity to tone/culture-war issues make them the ultimate bellwether for competitive House and Senate races nationwide.

unsure.label: White Working Class
unsure.subtitle: White, No college
unsure.rationale: The realignment story of the last decade. This group's shift from Obama to Trump remade the electoral map. Any erosion or consolidation in this bloc makes front-page news because it directly determines outcomes in PA, MI, WI, and Sun Belt exurbs.

unsure.label: Barstool Conservatives
unsure.subtitle: Male, Gen X or Millennials, No college or College completed, Republican or Other party
unsure.rationale: Culturally conservative but socially libertarian — anti-woke but pro-choice, pro-legal weed, pro-gambling. Credited with helping propel Trump to victory in 2024 through alternative media (Rogan, Barstool Sports, UFC). Not church-right but vibes-right. Their turnout and enthusiasm is a leading indicator of populist energy, and both parties are now scrambling to understand them.

unsure.label: Disaffected Young Men
unsure.subtitle: Male, Gen Z or Millennials, Other party affiliation
unsure.rationale: The emerging wildcard of American elections. Low-propensity, politically homeless, over-indexed on alternative media. Both parties competing for them. Their turnout alone can shift margins by 2-4 points in swing states.

unsure.label: Black Women of Faith
unsure.subtitle: Female, Black, Protestant, Democrat
unsure.rationale: The most reliable voting bloc in the Democratic coalition. When their turnout dips even slightly, Democrats lose. Media tracks their enthusiasm as a proxy for the health of the entire Democratic operation.

unsure.label: Hispanic Working Class
unsure.subtitle: Male, Hispanic, No college
unsure.rationale: The fastest-moving demographic in American politics. Their rightward shift in 2020 and 2024 caught both parties off guard. This group's trajectory determines whether the Sun Belt stays competitive or locks in for one party.

unsure.label: Evangelical Republicans
unsure.subtitle: Republican, Protestant
unsure.rationale: The floor of the Republican coalition. Their mobilization determines whether GOP candidates hit their targets in virtually every state. Post-Dobbs, media watches whether this group stays energized or fractures over candidate quality.

unsure.label: MAGA Country
unsure.subtitle: Rural, Republican, White, No college
unsure.rationale: The margin machine for Republicans. Their ever-increasing turnout in non-metro areas offsets Democratic gains in suburbs. Media covers them as the cultural heartbeat of the populist right. Any sign of turnout fatigue here is a major story.

unsure.label: TikTok Left
unsure.subtitle: Gen Z, Democrat
unsure.rationale: The energy wing of the Democratic base. Their enthusiasm on climate, student debt, and social issues translates to volunteer hours, small-dollar donations, and turnout in college towns. When disengaged, Democratic margins collapse.

unsure.label: Costco Centrists
unsure.subtitle: Suburban, Other party affiliation
unsure.rationale: The persuadable middle that both parties need. Not ideological, but they vote. Media uses them as the 'normal person' stand-in — decides based on gas prices, grocery costs, and vibes, not policy papers.

unsure.label: Tradwifes
unsure.subtitle: Female, Millennials or Gen Z, Republican, Protestant or Catholic, Suburban or Rural
unsure.rationale: A culturally ascendant archetype driven by social media. Embraces traditional gender roles, homemaking aesthetics, and conservative family values. Gained massive visibility in 2024 as a cultural counterpoint to progressive feminism. Media watches them because the tradwife trend was seen as a leading indicator of Trump's win among white women.

unsure.label: Young Black Men
unsure.subtitle: Male, Black, Gen Z
unsure.rationale: A small but symbolically enormous group. Any movement away from Democrats gets massive media coverage because it threatens a core assumption of the Democratic coalition. Even marginal shifts carry outsize narrative weight.

unsure.label: Country Club Republicans
unsure.subtitle: Republican, Suburban, Rich (125k+), College completed or Graduate degree
unsure.rationale: The Republican voters most likely to defect on candidate quality. Their movement in wealthy suburbs (Bucks County, Maricopa, Gwinnett) decided multiple swing states. If they hold for the GOP, the party is strong.
